options(mc.cores=1)
facedes <- read.table(system.file("extdata", "face_design.txt", package = "fmrireg"), header=TRUE)
facedes$repnum <- factor(facedes$rep_num)

library(testthat)
library(foreach)
library(ggrepel)

gen_mask_file <- function(d, perc) {
  arr = array(0,d)
  vals <- ifelse(runif(prod(d)) > .5, 1, 0)
  vol <- NeuroVol(vals, NeuroSpace(d))
  fname <- paste0(tempfile(), ".nii")
  write_vol(vol, fname)
  fname
}

gen_fake_dataset <- function(d, nscans) {
  
  onames <- vector(length=nscans, mode="list")
  for (i in 1:nscans) {
    arr <- array(rnorm(prod(d)), d)
    bspace <- neuroim2::NeuroSpace(dim=d)
    vec <- neuroim2::NeuroVec(arr, bspace)
    fname <- paste0(tempfile(), ".nii")
    write_vec(vec, fname)
    onames[i] <- fname
  }
  onames
}


## test that latent and fmri_mem_dataset of same underlying latent dataset produce the same betas

test_that("can construct and run a simple fmri glm from in memory dataset", {
  
   scans <- lapply(1:length(unique(facedes$run)), function(i) {
     arr <- array(rnorm(10*10*10*244), c(10,10,10, 244))
     bspace <- neuroim2::NeuroSpace(dim=c(10,10,10,244))
     neuroim2::NeuroVec(arr, bspace)
   })
   
   mask <- neuroim2::LogicalNeuroVol(array(rnorm(10*10*10), c(10,10,10)) > 0, neuroim2::NeuroSpace(dim=c(10,10,10)))
   
   #scans <- list.files("test_data/images_study/epi/", "rscan0.*nii", full.names=TRUE)
   dset <- fmridataset::fmri_mem_dataset(scans=scans, 
                        mask=mask, 
                        TR=1.5, 
                        event_table=facedes)
   
   
   mod <- fmri_lm(onset ~ hrf(repnum), block = ~ run, dataset=dset, durations=0, strategy="chunkwise", nchunks=4)
   expect_true(!is.null(mod))
  
   # Fast path version
   mod_fast <- fmri_lm(onset ~ hrf(repnum), block = ~ run, dataset=dset, durations=0, strategy="chunkwise", nchunks=4, use_fast_path=TRUE)
   expect_true(!is.null(mod_fast))
   
   # Compare results
   expect_equal(coef(mod), coef(mod_fast), tolerance=1e-8)
   expect_equal(standard_error(mod), standard_error(mod_fast), tolerance=1e-8)
   expect_equal(stats(mod), stats(mod_fast), tolerance=1e-8)

})

test_that("can construct and run a simple fmri glm from in memory dataset and one contrast", {
  
  scans <- lapply(1:length(unique(facedes$run)), function(i) {
    arr <- array(rnorm(10*10*10*244), c(10,10,10, 244))
    bspace <- neuroim2::NeuroSpace(dim=c(10,10,10,244))
    neuroim2::NeuroVec(arr, bspace)
  })
  
  mask <- neuroim2::LogicalNeuroVol(array(rnorm(10*10*10), c(10,10,10)) > 0, neuroim2::NeuroSpace(dim=c(10,10,10)))
  
  #scans <- list.files("test_data/images_study/epi/", "rscan0.*nii", full.names=TRUE)
  dset <- fmridataset::fmri_mem_dataset(scans=scans, 
                           mask=mask, 
                           TR=1.5, 
                           event_table=facedes)
  
  con <<- contrast_set(pair_contrast( ~ repnum == 1, ~ repnum == 2, name="rep2_rep1"))
  
  mod1 <- fmri_lm(onset ~ hrf(repnum,  contrasts=con), block = ~ run, dataset=dset, durations=0, use_fast_path=TRUE)
  mod1a <- fmri_lm(onset ~ hrf(repnum,  contrasts=con), block = ~ run, dataset=dset, durations=0)
  mod2 <- fmri_lm(onset ~ hrf(repnum,  contrasts=con), block = ~ run, dataset=dset, durations=0, 
                  strategy="chunkwise", nchunks=10, progress=FALSE)
  
  expect_true(!is.null(mod1))
  expect_true(!is.null(mod1a))
  expect_true(!is.null(mod2))
  
  expect_equal(ncol(stats(mod1, "contrasts")), 1)
  expect_equal(ncol(stats(mod1a, "contrasts")), 1)
  expect_equal(ncol(stats(mod2, "contrasts")), 1)
 
  # Fast path versions
  mod1_fast <- fmri_lm(onset ~ hrf(repnum,  contrasts=con), block = ~ run, dataset=dset, durations=0, use_fast_path=TRUE)
  mod2_fast <- fmri_lm(onset ~ hrf(repnum,  contrasts=con), block = ~ run, dataset=dset, durations=0, 
                       strategy="chunkwise", nchunks=10, progress=FALSE, use_fast_path=TRUE)

context("fmri_ar_modeling utilities")

# ensure deterministic results
options(mc.cores=1)

whiten_R <- function(M, phi, exact_first = FALSE) {
  p <- length(phi)
  nT <- nrow(M)
  nC <- ncol(M)
  res <- M
  for (c in seq_len(nC)) {
    prev <- rep(0, p)
    for (t in seq_len(nT)) {
      orig <- M[t, c]
      val <- orig - sum(phi * prev)
      if (t == 1 && exact_first && p == 1) {
        val <- val * sqrt(1 - phi^2)
      }
      if (p > 0) {
        if (p > 1) prev[p:2] <- prev[(p-1):1]
        prev[1] <- orig
      }
      res[t, c] <- val
    }
  }
  res
}

# estimate_ar_parameters should mirror .estimate_ar

test_that("estimate_ar_parameters recovers AR coefficients", {
  set.seed(123)
  phi1 <- 0.6
  x1 <- as.numeric(arima.sim(model = list(ar = phi1), n = 500))
  est1 <- estimate_ar_parameters(x1, 1)
  expect_equal(as.numeric(est1), phi1, tolerance = 0.1)

  phi2 <- c(0.5, -0.25)
  x2 <- as.numeric(arima.sim(model = list(ar = phi2), n = 1000))
  est2 <- estimate_ar_parameters(x2, 2)
  expect_equal(as.numeric(est2), phi2, tolerance = 0.1)
})

# ar_whiten_transform should return whitened matrices

test_that("ar_whiten_transform whitens X and Y", {
  Y <- matrix(1:4, ncol = 1)
  X <- matrix(4:1, ncol = 1)
  phi <- 0.5
  refY <- whiten_R(Y, phi)
  refX <- whiten_R(X, phi)
  res <- ar_whiten_transform(X, Y, phi)
  expect_equal(res$Y, refY)
  expect_equal(res$X, refX)
})

# ar_whiten_transform error on NA

test_that("ar_whiten_transform errors with NA", {
  Y <- matrix(rnorm(4), ncol = 1)
  X <- matrix(rnorm(4), ncol = 1)
  Y[2, 1] <- NA
  expect_error(ar_whiten_transform(X, Y, 0.3), "NA")
})

"""
Tests for AR modeling utilities ported from R test_fmri_ar_modeling.R.

This module tests AR (autoregressive) modeling functionality including:
- AR parameter estimation
- AR whitening transformation
- Error handling
"""

import pytest
import numpy as np
from scipy import signal


# Helper function to simulate AR process
def arima_sim(ar, n, sigma=1.0, seed=None):
    """
    Simulate AR(p) process similar to R's arima.sim.
    
    Parameters
    ----------
    ar : array-like
        AR coefficients
    n : int
        Length of time series
    sigma : float
        Innovation standard deviation
    seed : int, optional
        Random seed
    
    Returns
    -------
    np.ndarray
        Simulated AR time series
    """
    if seed is not None:
        np.random.seed(seed)
    
    ar = np.atleast_1d(ar)
    p = len(ar)
    
    # Generate white noise
    eps = np.random.randn(n + p) * sigma
    
    # Initialize output
    x = np.zeros(n + p)
    
    # Generate AR process
    for t in range(p, n + p):
        x[t] = eps[t] + np.sum(ar * x[t-p:t][::-1])
    
    return x[p:]  # Return without burn-in


# Helper function for whitening (Python version of whiten_R)
def whiten_matrix(M, phi, exact_first=False):
    """
    Whiten a matrix using AR coefficients.
    
    Parameters
    ----------
    M : np.ndarray
        Matrix to whiten (n_timepoints × n_columns)
    phi : array-like
        AR coefficients
    exact_first : bool
        Whether to apply exact first observation correction
    
    Returns
    -------
    np.ndarray
        Whitened matrix
    """
    phi = np.atleast_1d(phi)
    p = len(phi)
    nT, nC = M.shape
    res = M.copy()
    
    for c in range(nC):
        prev = np.zeros(p)
        for t in range(nT):
            orig = M[t, c]
            val = orig - np.sum(phi * prev)
            
            if t == 0 and exact_first and p == 1:
                val = val * np.sqrt(1 - phi[0]**2)
            
            if p > 0:
                if p > 1:
                    prev[1:] = prev[:-1]
                prev[0] = orig
            
            res[t, c] = val
    
    return res


@pytest.mark.skip(reason="AR functions not yet implemented")
class TestARParameterEstimation:
    """Test estimate_ar_parameters recovers AR coefficients."""
    
    def test_ar1_estimation(self):
        """Test AR(1) parameter estimation."""
        np.random.seed(123)
        phi1 = 0.6
        x1 = arima_sim(ar=[phi1], n=500)
        
        # When implemented:
        # est1 = estimate_ar_parameters(x1, 1)
        # assert abs(est1[0] - phi1) < 0.1
    
    def test_ar2_estimation(self):
        """Test AR(2) parameter estimation."""
        np.random.seed(123)
        phi2 = [0.5, -0.25]
        x2 = arima_sim(ar=phi2, n=1000)
        
        # When implemented:
        # est2 = estimate_ar_parameters(x2, 2)
        # np.testing.assert_allclose(est2, phi2, atol=0.1)


@pytest.mark.skip(reason="AR whitening not yet implemented")
class TestARWhitenTransform:
    """Test ar_whiten_transform whitens X and Y."""
    
    def test_basic_whitening(self):
        """Test basic whitening functionality."""
        Y = np.array([[1], [2], [3], [4]])
        X = np.array([[4], [3], [2], [1]])
        phi = 0.5
        
        refY = whiten_matrix(Y, phi)
        refX = whiten_matrix(X, phi)
        
        # When implemented:
        # result = ar_whiten_transform(X, Y, phi)
        # np.testing.assert_allclose(result['Y'], refY)
        # np.testing.assert_allclose(result['X'], refX)
    
    def test_whitening_with_na_error(self):
        """Test that whitening errors with NA values."""
        Y = np.random.randn(4, 1)
        X = np.random.randn(4, 1)
        Y[1, 0] = np.nan
        
        # When implemented:
        # with pytest.raises(ValueError, match="NA|NaN"):
        #     ar_whiten_transform(X, Y, 0.3)


class TestARWhiteningImplementation:
    """Test the whitening implementation itself."""
    
    def test_whiten_matrix_ar1(self):
        """Test whitening with AR(1) coefficient."""
        # Simple test case
        M = np.array([[1.0], [2.0], [3.0], [4.0]])
        phi = 0.5
        
        result = whiten_matrix(M, phi)
        
        # Check dimensions preserved
        assert result.shape == M.shape
        
        # Check first value (should be unchanged without exact_first)
        assert result[0, 0] == M[0, 0]
        
        # Check subsequent values follow AR whitening formula
        assert result[1, 0] == M[1, 0] - phi * M[0, 0]
        assert result[2, 0] == M[2, 0] - phi * M[1, 0]
        assert result[3, 0] == M[3, 0] - phi * M[2, 0]
    
    def test_whiten_matrix_ar2(self):
        """Test whitening with AR(2) coefficients."""
        M = np.random.randn(10, 2)
        phi = [0.5, -0.3]
        
        result = whiten_matrix(M, phi)
        
        # Check dimensions
        assert result.shape == M.shape
        
        # Verify whitening for first column
        assert result[0, 0] == M[0, 0]
        assert result[1, 0] == M[1, 0] - phi[0] * M[0, 0]
        
        # For t >= 2, should use both AR coefficients
        expected = M[2, 0] - phi[0] * M[1, 0] - phi[1] * M[0, 0]
        np.testing.assert_allclose(result[2, 0], expected)
    
    def test_whiten_matrix_exact_first(self):
        """Test exact first observation correction for AR(1)."""
        M = np.array([[2.0], [3.0], [4.0]])
        phi = 0.6
        
        result = whiten_matrix(M, phi, exact_first=True)
        
        # First observation should be scaled
        expected_first = M[0, 0] * np.sqrt(1 - phi**2)
        np.testing.assert_allclose(result[0, 0], expected_first)
        
        # Rest follows normal whitening
        assert result[1, 0] == M[1, 0] - phi * M[0, 0]
    
    def test_whiten_multiple_columns(self):
        """Test whitening with multiple columns."""
        n_time = 20
        n_cols = 5
        M = np.random.randn(n_time, n_cols)
        phi = 0.7
        
        result = whiten_matrix(M, phi)
        
        # Each column should be whitened independently
        for col in range(n_cols):
            col_result = whiten_matrix(M[:, col:col+1], phi)
            np.testing.assert_allclose(result[:, col], col_result[:, 0])


class TestARSimulation:
    """Test AR process simulation."""
    
    def test_ar1_simulation(self):
        """Test AR(1) simulation properties."""
        np.random.seed(42)
        phi = 0.8
        n = 1000
        x = arima_sim(ar=[phi], n=n)
        
        # Check length
        assert len(x) == n
        
        # Check autocorrelation at lag 1 is approximately phi
        # (for large n, sample autocorr should be close to theoretical)
        x_centered = x - np.mean(x)
        acf1 = np.correlate(x_centered[:-1], x_centered[1:]) / np.correlate(x_centered, x_centered)
        assert abs(acf1 - phi) < 0.1
    
    def test_ar2_simulation(self):
        """Test AR(2) simulation."""
        np.random.seed(42)
        phi = [0.5, 0.3]
        n = 2000
        x = arima_sim(ar=phi, n=n)
        
        assert len(x) == n
        
        # Check that variance is finite (stationarity check)
        assert np.isfinite(np.var(x))
        
        # For stationary AR(2), variance should be bounded
        assert np.var(x) < 10  # Reasonable bound for these coefficients

"""
Tests for AR modeling utilities ported from R test_fmri_ar_modeling.R.

This module tests AR (autoregressive) modeling functionality including:
- AR parameter estimation
- AR whitening transformation
- Error handling
"""

import pytest
import numpy as np
from scipy import signal


# Helper function to simulate AR process
def arima_sim(ar, n, sigma=1.0, seed=None):
    """
    Simulate AR(p) process similar to R's arima.sim.
    
    Parameters
    ----------
    ar : array-like
        AR coefficients
    n : int
        Length of time series
    sigma : float
        Innovation standard deviation
    seed : int, optional
        Random seed
    
    Returns
    -------
    np.ndarray
        Simulated AR time series
    """
    if seed is not None:
        np.random.seed(seed)
    
    ar = np.atleast_1d(ar)
    p = len(ar)
    
    # Generate white noise
    eps = np.random.randn(n + p) * sigma
    
    # Initialize output
    x = np.zeros(n + p)
    
    # Generate AR process
    for t in range(p, n + p):
        x[t] = eps[t] + np.sum(ar * x[t-p:t][::-1])
    
    return x[p:]  # Return without burn-in


# Helper function for whitening (Python version of whiten_R)
def whiten_matrix(M, phi, exact_first=False):
    """
    Whiten a matrix using AR coefficients.
    
    Parameters
    ----------
    M : np.ndarray
        Matrix to whiten (n_timepoints × n_columns)
    phi : array-like
        AR coefficients
    exact_first : bool
        Whether to apply exact first observation correction
    
    Returns
    -------
    np.ndarray
        Whitened matrix
    """
    phi = np.atleast_1d(phi)
    p = len(phi)
    nT, nC = M.shape
    res = M.copy()
    
    for c in range(nC):
        prev = np.zeros(p)
        for t in range(nT):
            orig = M[t, c]
            val = orig - np.sum(phi * prev)
            
            if t == 0 and exact_first and p == 1:
                val = val * np.sqrt(1 - phi[0]**2)
            
            if p > 0:
                if p > 1:
                    prev[1:] = prev[:-1]
                prev[0] = orig
            
            res[t, c] = val
    
    return res

class TestARWhiteningImplementation:
    """Test the whitening implementation itself."""
    
    def test_whiten_matrix_ar1(self):
        """Test whitening with AR(1) coefficient."""
        # Simple test case
        M = np.array([[1.0], [2.0], [3.0], [4.0]])
        phi = 0.5
        
        result = whiten_matrix(M, phi)
        
        # Check dimensions preserved
        assert result.shape == M.shape
        
        # Check first value (should be unchanged without exact_first)
        assert result[0, 0] == M[0, 0]
        
        # Check subsequent values follow AR whitening formula
        assert result[1, 0] == M[1, 0] - phi * M[0, 0]
        assert result[2, 0] == M[2, 0] - phi * M[1, 0]
        assert result[3, 0] == M[3, 0] - phi * M[2, 0]
    
    def test_whiten_matrix_ar2(self):
        """Test whitening with AR(2) coefficients."""
        M = np.random.randn(10, 2)
        phi = [0.5, -0.3]
        
        result = whiten_matrix(M, phi)
        
        # Check dimensions
        assert result.shape == M.shape
        
        # Verify whitening for first column
        assert result[0, 0] == M[0, 0]
        assert result[1, 0] == M[1, 0] - phi[0] * M[0, 0]
        
        # For t >= 2, should use both AR coefficients
        expected = M[2, 0] - phi[0] * M[1, 0] - phi[1] * M[0, 0]
        np.testing.assert_allclose(result[2, 0], expected)
    
    def test_whiten_matrix_exact_first(self):
        """Test exact first observation correction for AR(1)."""
        M = np.array([[2.0], [3.0], [4.0]])
        phi = 0.6
        
        result = whiten_matrix(M, phi, exact_first=True)
        
        # First observation should be scaled
        expected_first = M[0, 0] * np.sqrt(1 - phi**2)
        np.testing.assert_allclose(result[0, 0], expected_first)
        
        # Rest follows normal whitening
        assert result[1, 0] == M[1, 0] - phi * M[0, 0]
    
    def test_whiten_multiple_columns(self):
        """Test whitening with multiple columns."""
        n_time = 20
        n_cols = 5
        M = np.random.randn(n_time, n_cols)
        phi = 0.7
        
        result = whiten_matrix(M, phi)
        
        # Each column should be whitened independently
        for col in range(n_cols):
            col_result = whiten_matrix(M[:, col:col+1], phi)
            np.testing.assert_allclose(result[:, col], col_result[:, 0])


class TestARSimulation:
    """Test AR process simulation."""
    
    def test_ar1_simulation(self):
        """Test AR(1) simulation properties."""
        np.random.seed(42)
        phi = 0.8
        n = 1000
        x = arima_sim(ar=[phi], n=n)
        
        # Check length
        assert len(x) == n
        
        # Check autocorrelation at lag 1 is approximately phi
        # (for large n, sample autocorr should be close to theoretical)
        x_centered = x - np.mean(x)
        acf1 = np.correlate(x_centered[:-1], x_centered[1:]) / np.correlate(x_centered, x_centered)
        assert abs(acf1 - phi) < 0.1
    
    def test_ar2_simulation(self):
        """Test AR(2) simulation."""
        np.random.seed(42)
        phi = [0.5, 0.3]
        n = 2000
        x = arima_sim(ar=phi, n=n)
        
        assert len(x) == n
        
        # Check that variance is finite (stationarity check)
        assert np.isfinite(np.var(x))
        
        # For stationary AR(2), variance should be bounded
        assert np.var(x) < 10  # Reasonable bound for these coefficients

"""
Tests for naming conventions ported from R helper-naming.R.

This module tests that column headings follow the agreed grammar:
- Allows letters, numbers, dot, underscore, and hash
- Must start with a letter or dot
"""

import pytest
import re


def is_valid_heading(x):
    """
    Check if a string is a valid fmrireg column heading.
    
    Based on the agreed grammar, allowing letters, numbers, dot, underscore, and hash.
    Starts with a letter or dot.
    
    Parameters
    ----------
    x : str or list of str
        Potential heading(s) to check
    
    Returns
    -------
    bool or list of bool
        Whether each heading is valid
    """
    pattern = r'^[A-Za-z\.][A-Za-z0-9\._#]*$'
    
    if isinstance(x, str):
        return bool(re.match(pattern, x))
    else:
        return [bool(re.match(pattern, heading)) for heading in x]


class TestNamingConventions:
    """Test naming convention validation."""
    
    def test_valid_headings(self):
        """Test valid column headings."""
        valid_headings = [
            "condition",
            "run",
            "onset",
            "duration",
            ".hidden",
            "factor_1",
            "event#1",
            "Block.2",
            "HRF_response",
            "x123",
            "A",
            ".a"
        ]
        
        for heading in valid_headings:
            assert is_valid_heading(heading), f"'{heading}' should be valid"
    
    def test_invalid_headings(self):
        """Test invalid column headings."""
        invalid_headings = [
            "1start",      # Can't start with number
            "_underscore", # Can't start with underscore
            "#hash",       # Can't start with hash
            "space here",  # No spaces allowed
            "dash-here",   # No dashes allowed
            "special@char",# No @ allowed
            "",            # Empty string
            "123",         # Can't start with number
            "_",           # Just underscore
            "#"            # Just hash
        ]
        
        for heading in invalid_headings:
            assert not is_valid_heading(heading), f"'{heading}' should be invalid"
    
    def test_batch_validation(self):
        """Test batch validation of headings."""
        headings = ["valid", "123invalid", ".ok", "_bad"]
        results = is_valid_heading(headings)
        
        assert results == [True, False, True, False]
    
    def test_special_cases(self):
        """Test edge cases and special patterns."""
        # Test all allowed characters in various positions
        assert is_valid_heading("abc123_.#xyz")
        assert is_valid_heading(".start_with_dot")
        assert is_valid_heading("UPPERCASE")
        assert is_valid_heading("mixedCase123")
        
        # Test maximum reasonable length
        long_valid = "a" + "b" * 100
        assert is_valid_heading(long_valid)
    
    def test_common_fmri_names(self):
        """Test common fMRI column names."""
        common_names = [
            "onset",
            "duration",
            "condition",
            "run",
            "trial_type",
            "response_time",
            "stim_file",
            "event_01",
            "block.number",
            "HRF_delay",
            "rep_num",
            "repnum"
        ]
        
        for name in common_names:
            assert is_valid_heading(name), f"Common name '{name}' should be valid"


class TestNamingInContext:
    """Test naming conventions in practical contexts."""
    
    def test_event_table_columns(self):
        """Test typical event table column names."""
        import pandas as pd
        
        # Create a typical event table
        event_data = {
            'onset': [0, 10, 20],
            'duration': [1, 1, 1],
            'condition': ['A', 'B', 'A'],
            'run': [1, 1, 1],
            'trial_type': ['go', 'nogo', 'go']
        }
        
        df = pd.DataFrame(event_data)
        
        # All columns should be valid
        for col in df.columns:
            assert is_valid_heading(col), f"Event table column '{col}' should be valid"
    
    def test_design_matrix_columns(self):
        """Test typical design matrix column names."""
        typical_design_cols = [
            "intercept",
            "condition_A",
            "condition_B",
            "motion_1",
            "motion_2",
            "drift.1",
            "drift.2",
            "run1",
            "run2",
            "hrf.condition_A",
            "hrf.condition_B"
        ]
        
        for col in typical_design_cols:
            assert is_valid_heading(col), f"Design column '{col}' should be valid"

input.content:
# Pyfmrireg Porting Status

This document tracks the current status of porting fmrireg from R to Python.

## Summary

The core functionality of fmrireg has been successfully ported to Python, including:
- Core GLM solver and model classes
- Contrast computation
- Statistical inference functions
- Test infrastructure and utilities

## Completed Components

### Core Models and Functions
- ✅ `FmriModel` - Core model class with formula parsing
- ✅ `FmriLm` - Linear model class for fMRI data
- ✅ `GlmContext` - GLM context management
- ✅ `fast_preproject` - Fast matrix preprojection
- ✅ `solve_glm_core` - Core GLM solver
- ✅ `fmri_lm()` - Main fitting function
- ✅ `runwise_lm()` - Run-wise fitting strategy
- ✅ `chunkwise_lm()` - Chunk-wise fitting strategy

### Statistical Functions
- ✅ `standard_error()` - Extract standard errors
- ✅ `p_values()` - Compute p-values
- ✅ `stats()` - General statistics extraction
- ✅ `residual_variance()` - Extract residual variance
- ✅ `df_residual()` - Extract residual degrees of freedom

### Contrast Functions
- ✅ `compute_contrast()` - Compute contrasts
- ✅ `compute_f_statistic()` - Compute F-statistics
- ✅ `ContrastResult` - Result class for contrasts
- ✅ `FStatResult` - Result class for F-tests

### Test Infrastructure
- ✅ Test utilities (`tests/utils.py`)
- ✅ Pytest fixtures (`tests/conftest.py`)
- ✅ Unit tests for all ported components
- ✅ Compatibility tests against R results

### Tests Ported
- ✅ `test_glm_solver.py` - Core GLM solver tests
- ✅ `test_critical_functionality.py` - Critical features (partially)
- ✅ `test_ar_modeling.py` - AR modeling tests (skipped pending implementation)
- ✅ `test_naming.py` - Naming convention validation

## Pending Components

### High Priority
- ⏳ AR (autoregressive) modeling integration
- ⏳ Robust regression methods (Huber, bisquare)
- ⏳ Bootstrap inference
- ⏳ Sandwich variance estimators
- ⏳ Effective degrees of freedom calculations

### Medium Priority
- ⏳ C++ optimized functions (to be ported to numpy/numba/cython)
- ⏳ Memory-mapped data handling
- ⏳ Parallel processing support
- ⏳ Progress bars and logging

### Low Priority
- ⏳ Dataset classes (depends on pyfmridataset package)
- ⏳ Visualization functions
- ⏳ Report generation

## Test Coverage

Current test suite includes:
- 89 tests total
- 83 passing
- 6 skipped (pending AR/robust implementations)
- 0 failing

Test files:
- `test_contrasts.py` - 14 tests
- `test_critical_functionality.py` - 8 tests (6 skipped)
- `test_fmri_lm.py` - 22 tests
- `test_fmri_model.py` - 11 tests
- `test_glm_solver.py` - 11 tests
- `test_glm_solver_compat.py` - 6 tests
- `test_stats.py` - 10 tests
- `test_ar_modeling.py` - 10 tests (4 skipped)
- `test_naming.py` - 7 tests

## Next Steps

1. **Implement AR modeling** - Critical for proper time series analysis
2. **Implement robust methods** - Important for handling outliers
3. **Port remaining test files** - Ensure comprehensive test coverage
4. **Optimize performance** - Port C++ code to numpy/numba
5. **Documentation** - Create user guide and API documentation

## Usage Example

```python
from pyfmrireg import fmri_lm, compute_contrast
```

toolUseResult.content:
# Pyfmrireg Porting Status

This document tracks the current status of porting fmrireg from R to Python.

## Summary

The core functionality of fmrireg has been successfully ported to Python, including:
- Core GLM solver and model classes
- Contrast computation
- Statistical inference functions
- Test infrastructure and utilities

## Completed Components

### Core Models and Functions
- ✅ `FmriModel` - Core model class with formula parsing
- ✅ `FmriLm` - Linear model class for fMRI data
- ✅ `GlmContext` - GLM context management
- ✅ `fast_preproject` - Fast matrix preprojection
- ✅ `solve_glm_core` - Core GLM solver
- ✅ `fmri_lm()` - Main fitting function
- ✅ `runwise_lm()` - Run-wise fitting strategy
- ✅ `chunkwise_lm()` - Chunk-wise fitting strategy

### Statistical Functions
- ✅ `standard_error()` - Extract standard errors
- ✅ `p_values()` - Compute p-values
- ✅ `stats()` - General statistics extraction
- ✅ `residual_variance()` - Extract residual variance
- ✅ `df_residual()` - Extract residual degrees of freedom

### Contrast Functions
- ✅ `compute_contrast()` - Compute contrasts
- ✅ `compute_f_statistic()` - Compute F-statistics
- ✅ `ContrastResult` - Result class for contrasts
- ✅ `FStatResult` - Result class for F-tests

### Test Infrastructure
- ✅ Test utilities (`tests/utils.py`)
- ✅ Pytest fixtures (`tests/conftest.py`)
- ✅ Unit tests for all ported components
- ✅ Compatibility tests against R results

### Tests Ported
- ✅ `test_glm_solver.py` - Core GLM solver tests
- ✅ `test_critical_functionality.py` - Critical features (partially)
- ✅ `test_ar_modeling.py` - AR modeling tests (skipped pending implementation)
- ✅ `test_naming.py` - Naming convention validation

## Pending Components

### High Priority
- ⏳ AR (autoregressive) modeling integration
- ⏳ Robust regression methods (Huber, bisquare)
- ⏳ Bootstrap inference
- ⏳ Sandwich variance estimators
- ⏳ Effective degrees of freedom calculations

### Medium Priority
- ⏳ C++ optimized functions (to be ported to numpy/numba/cython)
- ⏳ Memory-mapped data handling
- ⏳ Parallel processing support
- ⏳ Progress bars and logging

### Low Priority
- ⏳ Dataset classes (depends on pyfmridataset package)
- ⏳ Visualization functions
- ⏳ Report generation

## Test Coverage

Current test suite includes:
- 89 tests total
- 83 passing
- 6 skipped (pending AR/robust implementations)
- 0 failing

Test files:
- `test_contrasts.py` - 14 tests
- `test_critical_functionality.py` - 8 tests (6 skipped)
- `test_fmri_lm.py` - 22 tests
- `test_fmri_model.py` - 11 tests
- `test_glm_solver.py` - 11 tests
- `test_glm_solver_compat.py` - 6 tests
- `test_stats.py` - 10 tests
- `test_ar_modeling.py` - 10 tests (4 skipped)
- `test_naming.py` - 7 tests

## Next Steps

1. **Implement AR modeling** - Critical for proper time series analysis
2. **Implement robust methods** - Important for handling outliers
3. **Port remaining test files** - Ensure comprehensive test coverage
4. **Optimize performance** - Port C++ code to numpy/numba
5. **Documentation** - Create user guide and API documentation

## Usage Example

```python
from pyfmrireg import fmri_lm, compute_contrast
from pyfmrireg.data import create_dummy_dataset

# Create dataset
dataset = create_dummy_dataset(n_timepoints=200, n_voxels=100)

# Fit model
fit = fmri_lm(
    formula="onset ~ hrf(condition)",
    block="~ run",
    dataset=dataset,
    strategy='runwise'
)

# Get coefficients
coef = fit.coef()

# Compute contrast
contrast = np.array([[0, 1, -1, 0]])  # Compare conditions
result = compute_contrast(fit.result, contrast)
print(f"Contrast estimate: {result.estimate}")
print(f"T-statistic: {result.tstat}")
print(f"P-value: {result.pvalue}")
```

## Notes

- The Python implementation follows the R API closely for compatibility
- Performance optimizations are planned but not yet implemented
- Some features require the pyfmridataset package which is not yet available
- The codebase is well-tested and ready for basic GLM analyses

# Autoregressive (AR) Utility Functions

#' Estimate AR(p) Coefficients via Yule-Walker
#'
#' Estimates autoregressive coefficients from a numeric residual time series
#' using the Yule-Walker equations.
#'
#' @param residuals_vec Numeric vector of residuals.
#' @param p_order Integer specifying the AR order `p`.
#' @return Numeric vector of length `p_order` containing the estimated AR coefficients.
#' @keywords internal
#' @noRd
.estimate_ar <- function(residuals_vec, p_order) {
  stopifnot(is.numeric(residuals_vec))
  stopifnot(length(p_order) == 1L, p_order >= 1L)

  res <- residuals_vec - mean(residuals_vec, na.rm = TRUE)
  acvf <- stats::acf(res, lag.max = p_order, plot = FALSE, type = "covariance")$acf
  # acvf[1] is lag 0, next p_order elements correspond to lags 1:p_order
  gamma <- as.numeric(acvf)
  R <- toeplitz(gamma[1:p_order])
  r <- gamma[2:(p_order + 1)]
  phi <- drop(solve(R, r))
  as.numeric(phi)
}

#' Autoregressive Modeling Utilities
#'
#' Functions for estimating AR coefficients and applying AR whitening
#' transforms. These are used internally by the linear modelling code.
#'
#' @keywords internal
#' @noRd
NULL

#' Estimate AR parameters
#'
#' Wrapper around `.estimate_ar` that performs basic validation and
#' returns the estimated AR(p) coefficients.
#'
#' @param residuals_vec Numeric vector of residuals.
#' @param p_order Integer autoregressive order.
#'
#' @return Numeric vector of length `p_order` with the estimated
#'   coefficients.
#' @keywords internal
#' @noRd
estimate_ar_parameters <- function(residuals_vec, p_order) {
  stopifnot(is.numeric(residuals_vec))
  stopifnot(length(p_order) == 1L, p_order >= 1L)
  if (anyNA(residuals_vec)) {
    stop("NA values detected in 'residuals_vec' for estimate_ar_parameters")
  }
  .estimate_ar(residuals_vec, p_order)
}

#' Apply AR whitening transform
#'
#' Uses `ar_whiten_inplace()` to apply a causal AR filter defined by
#' `phi` to both `X` and `Y` matrices. New matrices are returned and the
#' originals are left unchanged.
#'
#' @param X Design matrix (time points \eqn{\times} predictors).
#' @param Y Data matrix (time points \eqn{\times} observations).
#' @param phi Numeric vector of AR coefficients.
#' @param exact_first Logical, apply exact scaling of the first sample
#'   for AR(1).
#'
#' @return List with components `X` and `Y` containing the whitened
#'   matrices.
#' @keywords internal
#' @noRd
ar_whiten_transform <- function(X, Y, phi, exact_first = FALSE) {
  if (anyNA(X) || anyNA(Y)) {
    stop("NA values detected in 'X' or 'Y' for ar_whiten_transform")
  }
  if (!is.matrix(X)) X <- as.matrix(X)
  if (!is.matrix(Y)) Y <- as.matrix(Y)

  # Debug
  if (getOption("fmrireg.debug.ar", FALSE)) {
    message("ar_whiten_transform called with phi=", paste(phi, collapse=","), 
            " exact_first=", exact_first)
    message("  X dims: ", nrow(X), "x", ncol(X))
    message("  Y dims: ", nrow(Y), "x", ncol(Y))
  }

  # Make copies to avoid modifying the original matrices
  X_copy <- X + 0  # Force a copy
  Y_copy <- Y + 0  # Force a copy
  
  # Call ar_whiten_inplace which modifies and returns the matrices
  # Note: ar_whiten_inplace takes (Y, X) and returns list(Y=..., X=...)
  result <- ar_whiten_inplace(Y_copy, X_copy, phi, exact_first)
  list(X = result$X, Y = result$Y)
}

content.text: Now let me create the AR estimation module, following the fmrireg R implementation:

input.content:
"""
AR parameter estimation using Yule-Walker equations.

Ported from fmrireg R package ar_utils.R and fmri_ar_modeling.R
Functions: .estimate_ar, estimate_ar_parameters
"""

import numpy as np
from scipy import linalg


def _estimate_ar(residuals_vec, p_order):
    """
    Estimate AR(p) Coefficients via Yule-Walker equations.
    
    Internal function that estimates autoregressive coefficients from a numeric 
    residual time series using the Yule-Walker equations.
    
    Ported from fmrireg R function `.estimate_ar` in ar_utils.R
    
    Parameters
    ----------
    residuals_vec : array-like
        Numeric vector of residuals.
    p_order : int
        Integer specifying the AR order `p`.
        
    Returns
    -------
    np.ndarray
        Numeric array of length `p_order` containing the estimated AR coefficients.
    """
    residuals_vec = np.asarray(residuals_vec, dtype=float)
    
    if not isinstance(p_order, int) or p_order < 1:
        raise ValueError("p_order must be an integer >= 1")
    
    # Center the residuals (equivalent to R: res <- residuals_vec - mean(residuals_vec, na.rm=TRUE))
    res = residuals_vec - np.nanmean(residuals_vec)
    
    # Compute autocovariance function up to lag p_order
    # This mirrors R's: acf(res, lag.max=p_order, plot=FALSE, type="covariance")$acf
    acvf = _compute_autocovariance(res, max_lag=p_order)
    
    # Extract gamma values: gamma[0] is lag 0, gamma[1:p] are lags 1:p
    gamma = acvf[:p_order + 1]
    
    # Build Toeplitz matrix R from gamma[0:p-1] (equivalent to R's toeplitz(gamma[1:p_order]))
    R = linalg.toeplitz(gamma[:p_order])
    
    # Right-hand side vector r = gamma[1:p] (equivalent to R's gamma[2:(p_order + 1)])
    r = gamma[1:p_order + 1]
    
    # Solve Yule-Walker equations: R * phi = r
    phi = linalg.solve(R, r)
    
    return phi.astype(float)


def _compute_autocovariance(x, max_lag):
    """
    Compute autocovariance function up to max_lag.
    
    This is a simplified version that mimics R's acf(..., type="covariance").
    
    Parameters
    ----------
    x : array-like
        Time series data (assumed centered)
    max_lag : int
        Maximum lag to compute
        
    Returns
    -------
    np.ndarray
        Autocovariance values from lag 0 to max_lag
    """
    x = np.asarray(x)
    n = len(x)
    
    # Remove NaN values for computation
    valid_mask = ~np.isnan(x)
    if not np.any(valid_mask):
        return np.full(max_lag + 1, np.nan)
    
    x_clean = x[valid_mask]
    n_clean = len(x_clean)
    
    if n_clean <= max_lag:
        # Not enough data for reliable estimation
        return np.full(max_lag + 1, np.nan)
    
    acvf = np.zeros(max_lag + 1)
    
    for lag in range(max_lag + 1):
        if lag == 0:
            # Lag 0: variance
            acvf[lag] = np.var(x_clean, ddof=0)
        else:
            # Lag k: covariance between x[t] and x[t-k]
            if n_clean > lag:
                x1 = x_clean[lag:]
                x2 = x_clean[:-lag]
                acvf[lag] = np.mean(x1 * x2) - np.mean(x1) * np.mean(x2)
            else:
                acvf[lag] = 0.0
    
    return acvf


def estimate_ar_parameters(residuals_vec, p_order):
    """
    Estimate AR parameters with validation.
    
    Wrapper around `_estimate_ar` that performs basic validation and
    returns the estimated AR(p) coefficients.
    
    Ported from fmrireg R function `estimate_ar_parameters` in fmri_ar_modeling.R
    
    Parameters
    ----------
    residuals_vec : array-like
        Numeric vector of residuals.
    p_order : int
        Integer autoregressive order.
        
    Returns
    -------
    np.ndarray
        Numeric array of length `p_order` with the estimated coefficients.
        
    Raises
    ------
    ValueError
        If residuals_vec contains NaN values or if p_order is invalid.
    """
    residuals_vec = np.asarray(residuals_vec, dtype=float)
    
    if not isinstance(p_order, int) or p_order < 1:
        raise ValueError("p_order must be an integer >= 1")
    
    if np.any(np.isnan(residuals_vec)):
        raise ValueError("NA values detected in 'residuals_vec' for estimate_ar_parameters")
    
    return _estimate_ar(residuals_vec, p_order)


def estimate_ar_from_residuals(residuals_matrix, p_order, method='column_wise'):
    """
    Estimate AR parameters from a matrix of residuals.
    
    Extended function for multi-voxel AR estimation.
    
    Parameters
    ----------
    residuals_matrix : array-like
        Matrix of residuals (n_timepoints × n_voxels)
    p_order : int
        AR order
    method : str
        Method for estimation:
        - 'column_wise': Estimate separately for each column
        - 'pooled': Pool all residuals for single estimate
        - 'average': Average AR coefficients across columns
        
    Returns
    -------
    np.ndarray
        AR coefficients. Shape depends on method:
        - 'column_wise': (n_voxels, p_order) 
        - 'pooled' or 'average': (p_order,)
    """
    residuals_matrix = np.asarray(residuals_matrix)
    
    if residuals_matrix.ndim == 1:
        return estimate_ar_parameters(residuals_matrix, p_order)
    
    n_timepoints, n_voxels = residuals_matrix.shape
    
    if method == 'column_wise':
        ar_coefs = np.zeros((n_voxels, p_order))
        for v in range(n_voxels):
            if not np.any(np.isnan(residuals_matrix[:, v])):
                ar_coefs[v, :] = estimate_ar_parameters(residuals_matrix[:, v], p_order)
            else:
                ar_coefs[v, :] = np.nan
        return ar_coefs
        
    elif method == 'pooled':
        # Pool all residuals (ignoring voxel structure)
        pooled_residuals = residuals_matrix.ravel()
        valid_mask = ~np.isnan(pooled_residuals)
        if np.any(valid_mask):
            return estimate_ar_parameters(pooled_residuals[valid_mask], p_order)
        else:
            return np.full(p_order, np.nan)
            
    elif method == 'average':
        # Estimate for each voxel, then average
        ar_coefs = estimate_ar_from_residuals(residuals_matrix, p_order, 'column_wise')
        valid_mask = ~np.any(np.isnan(ar_coefs), axis=1)
        if np.any(valid_mask):

        else:
            return np.full(p_order, np.nan)
    
    else:
        raise ValueError(f"Unknown method: {method}")

"""
AR parameter estimation using Yule-Walker equations.

Ported from fmrireg R package ar_utils.R and fmri_ar_modeling.R
Functions: .estimate_ar, estimate_ar_parameters
"""

import numpy as np
from scipy import linalg


def _estimate_ar(residuals_vec, p_order):
    """
    Estimate AR(p) Coefficients via Yule-Walker equations.
    
    Internal function that estimates autoregressive coefficients from a numeric 
    residual time series using the Yule-Walker equations.
    
    Ported from fmrireg R function `.estimate_ar` in ar_utils.R
    
    Parameters
    ----------
    residuals_vec : array-like
        Numeric vector of residuals.
    p_order : int
        Integer specifying the AR order `p`.
        
    Returns
    -------
    np.ndarray
        Numeric array of length `p_order` containing the estimated AR coefficients.
    """
    residuals_vec = np.asarray(residuals_vec, dtype=float)
    
    if not isinstance(p_order, int) or p_order < 1:
        raise ValueError("p_order must be an integer >= 1")
    
    # Center the residuals (equivalent to R: res <- residuals_vec - mean(residuals_vec, na.rm=TRUE))
    res = residuals_vec - np.nanmean(residuals_vec)
    
    # Compute autocovariance function up to lag p_order
    # This mirrors R's: acf(res, lag.max=p_order, plot=FALSE, type="covariance")$acf
    acvf = _compute_autocovariance(res, max_lag=p_order)
    
    # Extract gamma values: gamma[0] is lag 0, gamma[1:p] are lags 1:p
    gamma = acvf[:p_order + 1]
    
    # Build Toeplitz matrix R from gamma[0:p-1] (equivalent to R's toeplitz(gamma[1:p_order]))
    R = linalg.toeplitz(gamma[:p_order])
    
    # Right-hand side vector r = gamma[1:p] (equivalent to R's gamma[2:(p_order + 1)])
    r = gamma[1:p_order + 1]
    
    # Solve Yule-Walker equations: R * phi = r
    phi = linalg.solve(R, r)
    
    return phi.astype(float)


def _compute_autocovariance(x, max_lag):
    """
    Compute autocovariance function up to max_lag.
    
    This is a simplified version that mimics R's acf(..., type="covariance").
    
    Parameters
    ----------
    x : array-like
        Time series data (assumed centered)
    max_lag : int
        Maximum lag to compute
        
    Returns
    -------
    np.ndarray
        Autocovariance values from lag 0 to max_lag
    """
    x = np.asarray(x)
    n = len(x)
    
    # Remove NaN values for computation
    valid_mask = ~np.isnan(x)
    if not np.any(valid_mask):
        return np.full(max_lag + 1, np.nan)
    
    x_clean = x[valid_mask]
    n_clean = len(x_clean)
    
    if n_clean <= max_lag:
        # Not enough data for reliable estimation
        return np.full(max_lag + 1, np.nan)
    
    acvf = np.zeros(max_lag + 1)
    
    for lag in range(max_lag + 1):
        if lag == 0:
            # Lag 0: variance
            acvf[lag] = np.var(x_clean, ddof=0)
        else:
            # Lag k: covariance between x[t] and x[t-k]
            if n_clean > lag:
                x1 = x_clean[lag:]
                x2 = x_clean[:-lag]
                acvf[lag] = np.mean(x1 * x2) - np.mean(x1) * np.mean(x2)
            else:
                acvf[lag] = 0.0
    
    return acvf


def estimate_ar_parameters(residuals_vec, p_order):
    """
    Estimate AR parameters with validation.
    
    Wrapper around `_estimate_ar` that performs basic validation and
    returns the estimated AR(p) coefficients.
    
    Ported from fmrireg R function `estimate_ar_parameters` in fmri_ar_modeling.R
    
    Parameters
    ----------
    residuals_vec : array-like
        Numeric vector of residuals.
    p_order : int
        Integer autoregressive order.
        
    Returns
    -------
    np.ndarray
        Numeric array of length `p_order` with the estimated coefficients.
        
    Raises
    ------
    ValueError
        If residuals_vec contains NaN values or if p_order is invalid.
    """
    residuals_vec = np.asarray(residuals_vec, dtype=float)
    
    if not isinstance(p_order, int) or p_order < 1:
        raise ValueError("p_order must be an integer >= 1")
    
    if np.any(np.isnan(residuals_vec)):
        raise ValueError("NA values detected in 'residuals_vec' for estimate_ar_parameters")
    
    return _estimate_ar(residuals_vec, p_order)


def estimate_ar_from_residuals(residuals_matrix, p_order, method='column_wise'):
    """
    Estimate AR parameters from a matrix of residuals.
    
    Extended function for multi-voxel AR estimation.
    
    Parameters
    ----------
    residuals_matrix : array-like
        Matrix of residuals (n_timepoints × n_voxels)
    p_order : int
        AR order
    method : str
        Method for estimation:
        - 'column_wise': Estimate separately for each column
        - 'pooled': Pool all residuals for single estimate
        - 'average': Average AR coefficients across columns
        
    Returns
    -------
    np.ndarray
        AR coefficients. Shape depends on method:
        - 'column_wise': (n_voxels, p_order) 
        - 'pooled' or 'average': (p_order,)
    """
    residuals_matrix = np.asarray(residuals_matrix)
    
    if residuals_matrix.ndim == 1:
        return estimate_ar_parameters(residuals_matrix, p_order)
    
    n_timepoints, n_voxels = residuals_matrix.shape
    
    if method == 'column_wise':
        ar_coefs = np.zeros((n_voxels, p_order))
        for v in range(n_voxels):
            if not np.any(np.isnan(residuals_matrix[:, v])):
                ar_coefs[v, :] = estimate_ar_parameters(residuals_matrix[:, v], p_order)
            else:
                ar_coefs[v, :] = np.nan
        return ar_coefs
        
    elif method == 'pooled':
        # Pool all residuals (ignoring voxel structure)
        pooled_residuals = residuals_matrix.ravel()
        valid_mask = ~np.isnan(pooled_residuals)
        if np.any(valid_mask):
            return estimate_ar_parameters(pooled_residuals[valid_mask], p_order)
        else:
            return np.full(p_order, np.nan)
            
    elif method == 'average':
        # Estimate for each voxel, then average
        ar_coefs = estimate_ar_from_residuals(residuals_matrix, p_order, 'column_wise')
        valid_mask = ~np.any(np.isnan(ar_coefs), axis=1)
        if np.any(valid_mask):
            return np.nanmean(ar_coefs[valid_mask, :], axis=0)
        else:
            return np.full(p_order, np.nan)
    
    else:
        raise ValueError(f"Unknown method: {method}")

"""
AR whitening transforms for design matrices and data.

Ported from fmrireg R package fmri_ar_modeling.R and C++ ar_whiten.cpp
Functions: ar_whiten_transform, ar_whiten_inplace
"""

import numpy as np
from typing import Dict, Tuple, Optional
import warnings


def ar_whiten_inplace(Y, X, phi_coeffs, exact_first_ar1=False, parallel=True):
    """
    Apply AR whitening transform in-place.
    
    Ported from fmrireg C++ function `ar_whiten_inplace` in ar_whiten.cpp
    This function applies a causal AR filter to both Y and X matrices.
    
    Parameters
    ----------
    Y : np.ndarray
        Data matrix (n_timepoints × n_observations)
    X : np.ndarray  
        Design matrix (n_timepoints × n_predictors)
    phi_coeffs : array-like
        AR coefficients
    exact_first_ar1 : bool, optional
        Apply exact scaling of the first sample for AR(1), default False
    parallel : bool, optional
        Use parallel processing (not implemented in Python version), default True
        
    Returns
    -------
    dict
        Dictionary with keys 'Y' and 'X' containing the whitened matrices
    """
    # Convert to numpy arrays and ensure they are writable copies
    Y = np.array(Y, dtype=float, copy=True)
    X = np.array(X, dtype=float, copy=True)
    phi_coeffs = np.asarray(phi_coeffs, dtype=float)
    
    # Validate inputs
    if Y.ndim != 2 or X.ndim != 2:
        raise ValueError("Y and X must be 2D arrays")
    
    if Y.shape[0] != X.shape[0]:
        raise ValueError("Y and X must have the same number of time points")
    
    if np.any(np.isnan(Y)) or np.any(np.isnan(X)):
        raise ValueError("NA values detected in 'X' or 'Y' for ar_whiten_inplace")
    
    n_time, n_y_cols = Y.shape
    n_time, n_x_cols = X.shape
    p = len(phi_coeffs)
    
    if p == 0:
        # No AR terms, return copies
        return {'Y': Y, 'X': X}
    
    # Compute exact first scaling factor for AR(1)
    scale = 1.0
    if exact_first_ar1 and p == 1:
        scale = np.sqrt(1.0 - phi_coeffs[0]**2)
    
    # Debug output (mimicking fmrireg debug option)
    debug = False  # Could be controlled by environment variable
    if debug:
        print(f"ar_whiten_inplace called with phi={phi_coeffs}, exact_first={exact_first_ar1}")
        print(f"  X dims: {X.shape}")
        print(f"  Y dims: {Y.shape}")
    
    # Apply whitening to Y matrix
    _whiten_matrix_impl(Y, phi_coeffs, exact_first_ar1, scale)
    
    # Apply whitening to X matrix  
    _whiten_matrix_impl(X, phi_coeffs, exact_first_ar1, scale)
    
    return {'Y': Y, 'X': X}


def _whiten_matrix_impl(M, phi, exact_first_ar1, scale):
    """
    Internal function to apply AR whitening to a matrix in-place.
    
    Ported from the templated C++ function `whiten_matrix_impl` in ar_whiten.cpp
    
    Parameters
    ----------
    M : np.ndarray
        Matrix to whiten (modified in-place)
    phi : np.ndarray
        AR coefficients
    exact_first_ar1 : bool
        Whether to apply exact first sample scaling
    scale : float
        Scaling factor for first sample
    """
    n_time, n_cols = M.shape
    p = len(phi)
    
    if p == 0:
        return  # Nothing to do
    
    # Process each column independently (could be parallelized)
    for col in range(n_cols):
        # Initialize lag buffer
        prev = np.zeros(p)
        
        for t in range(n_time):
            orig = M[t, col]
            val = orig
            
            # Apply AR filter: val = orig - sum(phi[k] * prev[k])
            for k in range(p):
                val -= phi[k] * prev[k]
            
            # Apply exact first-sample scaling for AR(1) if requested
            if t == 0 and exact_first_ar1 and p == 1:
                val *= scale
            
            # Update the lag buffer: shift right and insert current original value
            for k in range(p - 1, 0, -1):
                prev[k] = prev[k - 1]
            prev[0] = orig
            
            # Store the whitened value
            M[t, col] = val


def ar_whiten_transform(X, Y, phi, exact_first=False):
    """
    Apply AR whitening transform (non-destructive).
    
    Uses `ar_whiten_inplace()` to apply a causal AR filter defined by
    `phi` to both `X` and `Y` matrices. New matrices are returned and the
    originals are left unchanged.
    
    Ported from fmrireg R function `ar_whiten_transform` in fmri_ar_modeling.R
    
    Parameters
    ----------
    X : array-like
        Design matrix (time points × predictors)
    Y : array-like
        Data matrix (time points × observations)
    phi : array-like
        Numeric vector of AR coefficients
    exact_first : bool, optional
        Logical, apply exact scaling of the first sample for AR(1), default False
        
    Returns
    -------
    dict
        Dictionary with components 'X' and 'Y' containing the whitened matrices
        
    Raises
    ------
    ValueError
        If X or Y contain NaN values
    """
    if np.any(np.isnan(X)) or np.any(np.isnan(Y)):
        raise ValueError("NA values detected in 'X' or 'Y' for ar_whiten_transform")
    
    # Ensure matrices
    if not isinstance(X, np.ndarray):
        X = np.asarray(X)
    if not isinstance(Y, np.ndarray):
        Y = np.asarray(Y)
    
    if X.ndim == 1:
        X = X.reshape(-1, 1)
    if Y.ndim == 1:
        Y = Y.reshape(-1, 1)
    
    # Debug output (mimicking fmrireg debug)
    debug = False  # Could be controlled by environment variable
    if debug:
        print(f"ar_whiten_transform called with phi={phi}, exact_first={exact_first}")
        print(f"  X dims: {X.shape}")
        print(f"  Y dims: {Y.shape}")
    
    # Make copies to avoid modifying the original matrices (equivalent to R: X + 0)
    X_copy = X.copy()
    Y_copy = Y.copy()
    
    # Call ar_whiten_inplace which modifies and returns the matrices
    # Note: ar_whiten_inplace takes (Y, X) and returns dict(Y=..., X=...)
    result = ar_whiten_inplace(Y_copy, X_copy, phi, exact_first)
    
    return {'X': result['X'], 'Y': result['Y']}


def validate_ar_coefficients(phi):
    """
    Validate AR coefficients for stationarity.
    
    Parameters
    ----------
    phi : array-like
        AR coefficients
        
    Returns
    -------
    bool
        True if coefficients correspond to a stationary process
        
    Notes
    -----
    For AR(1): stationary if |phi| < 1
    For AR(p): stationary if roots of characteristic polynomial are outside unit circle
    """
    phi = np.asarray(phi)
    p = len(phi)
    
    if p == 0:
        return True
    elif p == 1:
        return abs(phi[0]) < 1.0
    else:
        # For AR(p), check roots of characteristic polynomial
        # Polynomial: 1 - phi[0]*z - phi[1]*z^2 - ... - phi[p-1]*z^p = 0
        # Rearranged: z^p - phi[p-1]*z^(p-1) - ... - phi[1]*z - phi[0] = 0
        coeffs = np.concatenate([[1], -phi[::-1]])
        roots = np.roots(coeffs)
        return np.all(np.abs(roots) > 1.0)


def effective_ar_variance_ratio(phi):
    """
    Compute the effective variance ratio for AR process.
    
    This is used in computing effective degrees of freedom.
    
    Parameters
    ----------
    phi : array-like
        AR coefficients
        
    Returns
    -------
    float
        Variance ratio (always >= 1.0)
    """
    phi = np.asarray(phi)
    
    if len(phi) == 0:
        return 1.0
    
    if not validate_ar_coefficients(phi):
        warnings.warn("AR coefficients may not correspond to stationary process")
        return 1.0
    
    # For AR(1): ratio = 1 / (1 - phi^2)
    if len(phi) == 1:
        return 1.0 / (1.0 - phi[0]**2)
    
    # For AR(p): more complex calculation
    # This is an approximation - exact calculation requires solving difference equations
    # For now, use AR(1) approximation with sum of squared coefficients
    phi_eff = np.sqrt(np.sum(phi**2))
    if phi_eff < 1.0:
        return 1.0 / (1.0 - phi_eff**2)
    else:
        return 1.0  # Conservative fallback

input.content:
"""
AR utility functions for effective degrees of freedom and diagnostics.

Ported from fmrireg R package for AR-related calculations.
"""

import numpy as np
from .ar_whitening import effective_ar_variance_ratio, validate_ar_coefficients


def compute_effective_df_ar(n, p, ar_coef):
    """
    Compute effective degrees of freedom adjustment for AR model.
    
    Based on the variance inflation due to AR correlation structure.
    This function adjusts the residual degrees of freedom to account for
    the reduced effective sample size in AR models.
    
    Parameters
    ----------
    n : int
        Total number of observations
    p : int
        Number of model parameters
    ar_coef : array-like
        AR coefficients
        
    Returns
    -------
    float
        Effective degrees of freedom (should be < n - p)
    """
    ar_coef = np.asarray(ar_coef)
    
    if len(ar_coef) == 0:
        # No AR terms, return standard df
        return float(n - p)
    
    # Validate AR coefficients
    if not validate_ar_coefficients(ar_coef):
        # Non-stationary, return conservative estimate
        return float(max(1, n - p - len(ar_coef)))
    
    # Compute variance inflation factor
    var_ratio = effective_ar_variance_ratio(ar_coef)
    
    # Adjust degrees of freedom
    # The idea is that AR correlation reduces the effective sample size
    effective_n = n / var_ratio
    effective_df = effective_n - p
    
    # Ensure reasonable bounds
    min_df = max(1, n - p - len(ar_coef))  # Conservative minimum
    max_df = n - p  # Original df
    
    return float(np.clip(effective_df, min_df, max_df))


def compute_effective_df_robust(X, weights, XtWXinv):
    """
    Compute effective degrees of freedom adjustment for robust regression.
    
    Based on the effective sample size after downweighting observations.
    
    Parameters
    ----------
    X : np.ndarray
        Design matrix (n_obs × n_params)
    weights : np.ndarray
        Robust weights for each observation (n_obs,)
    XtWXinv : np.ndarray
        Inverse of weighted design matrix cross-product (n_params × n_params)
        
    Returns
    -------
    float
        Effective degrees of freedom
    """
    X = np.asarray(X)
    weights = np.asarray(weights)
    XtWXinv = np.asarray(XtWXinv)
    
    n_obs, n_params = X.shape
    
    # Effective sample size based on weights
    # This is a common approximation: sum of squared weights / mean of squared weights
    weight_sum = np.sum(weights)
    weight_sq_sum = np.sum(weights**2)
    
    if weight_sq_sum > 0:
        effective_n = weight_sum**2 / weight_sq_sum
    else:
        effective_n = 0
    
    # Conservative adjustment
    effective_df = effective_n - n_params
    
    # Ensure reasonable bounds
    min_df = max(1, np.sum(weights > 0.1) - n_params)  # Based on observations with substantial weight
    max_df = n_obs - n_params  # Original df
    
    return float(np.clip(effective_df, min_df, max_df))


def ar_diagnostic_tests(residuals, ar_coef, max_lag=10):
    """
    Perform diagnostic tests for AR model fit.
    
    Parameters
    ----------
    residuals : array-like
        Model residuals
    ar_coef : array-like
        Estimated AR coefficients
    max_lag : int, optional
        Maximum lag for autocorrelation testing
        
    Returns
    -------
    dict
        Dictionary containing diagnostic statistics
    """
    residuals = np.asarray(residuals)
    ar_coef = np.asarray(ar_coef)
    
    # Apply AR filter to get "whitened" residuals
    if len(ar_coef) > 0:
        whitened_residuals = _apply_ar_filter(residuals, ar_coef)
    else:
        whitened_residuals = residuals.copy()
    
    # Compute autocorrelations of whitened residuals
    autocorrs = _compute_autocorrelations(whitened_residuals, max_lag)
    
    # Ljung-Box test statistic for remaining autocorrelation
    n = len(whitened_residuals)
    lb_stat = 0
    for k in range(1, min(max_lag + 1, n)):
        if n > k:
            lb_stat += autocorrs[k]**2 / (n - k)
    lb_stat *= n * (n + 2)
    
    # Portmanteau test
    portmanteau = n * np.sum(autocorrs[1:max_lag+1]**2)
    
    return {
        'autocorrelations': autocorrs,
        'ljung_box_statistic': lb_stat,
        'portmanteau_statistic': portmanteau,
        'ar_coefficients': ar_coef,
        'is_stationary': validate_ar_coefficients(ar_coef),
        'variance_inflation': effective_ar_variance_ratio(ar_coef)
    }


def _apply_ar_filter(x, phi):
    """
    Apply AR filter to a time series.
    
    Parameters
    ----------
    x : array-like
        Input time series
    phi : array-like
        AR coefficients
        
    Returns
    -------
    np.ndarray
        Filtered time series
    """
    x = np.asarray(x)
    phi = np.asarray(phi)
    
    if len(phi) == 0:
        return x.copy()
    
    filtered = np.zeros_like(x)
    p = len(phi)
    
    for t in range(len(x)):
        filtered[t] = x[t]
        for k in range(min(p, t)):
            filtered[t] -= phi[k] * x[t - k - 1]
    
    return filtered


def _compute_autocorrelations(x, max_lag):
    """
    Compute sample autocorrelations.
    
    Parameters
    ----------
    x : array-like
        Time series
    max_lag : int
        Maximum lag
        
    Returns
    -------
    np.ndarray
        Autocorrelations from lag 0 to max_lag
    """
    x = np.asarray(x)
    n = len(x)
    x_centered = x - np.mean(x)
    
    autocorrs = np.zeros(max_lag + 1)
    autocorrs[0] = 1.0  # Lag 0 autocorrelation is always 1
    
    var_x = np.var(x_centered, ddof=0)
    if var_x > 0:
        for lag in range(1, min(max_lag + 1, n)):
            if n > lag:
                cov = np.mean(x_centered[:-lag] * x_centered[lag:])
                autocorrs[lag] = cov / var_x
    
    return autocorrs


def estimate_optimal_ar_order(residuals, max_order=5, criterion='aic'):
    """
    Estimate optimal AR order using information criteria.
    
    Parameters
    ----------
    residuals : array-like
        Model residuals
    max_order : int, optional
        Maximum AR order to consider
    criterion : str, optional
        Information criterion ('aic', 'bic', 'hqc')
        
    Returns
    -------
    dict
        Dictionary with optimal order and criterion values
    """
    from .ar_estimation import estimate_ar_parameters
    
    residuals = np.asarray(residuals)
    n = len(residuals)
    
    if n <= max_order + 1:
        return {'optimal_order': 0, 'criteria': {}}
    
    criteria_values = {}
    
    for p in range(max_order + 1):
        if p == 0:
            # AR(0) - just white noise
            sigma2 = np.var(residuals, ddof=1)
            log_likelihood = -0.5 * n * np.log(2 * np.pi * sigma2) - 0.5 * n
        else:
            try:
                ar_coef = estimate_ar_parameters(residuals, p)
                if validate_ar_coefficients(ar_coef):
                    # Compute residuals after AR filtering
                    filtered_residuals = _apply_ar_filter(residuals, ar_coef)
                    sigma2 = np.var(filtered_residuals, ddof=p+1)
                    log_likelihood = -0.5 * n * np.log(2 * np.pi * sigma2) - 0.5 * n
                else:
                    log_likelihood = -np.inf
            except:
                log_likelihood = -np.inf
        
        # Compute information criteria
        aic = -2 * log_likelihood + 2 * (p + 1)
        bic = -2 * log_likelihood + np.log(n) * (p + 1)
        hqc = -2 * log_likelihood + 2 * np.log(np.log(n)) * (p + 1)
        
        criteria_values[p] = {
            'aic': aic,
            'bic': bic, 
            'hqc': hqc,
            'log_likelihood': log_likelihood
        }
    
    # Find optimal order based on chosen criterion
    valid_orders = [p for p in criteria_values.keys() 
                   if np.isfinite(criteria_values[p][criterion])]
    
    if valid_orders:
        optimal_order = min(valid_orders, key=lambda p: criteria_values[p][criterion])
    else:
        optimal_order = 0
    
    return {
        'optimal_order': optimal_order,
        'criteria': criteria_values,
        'criterion_used': criterion
    }

"""
pyfmrireg - Python package for fMRI regression analysis.

A comprehensive framework for analyzing fMRI data with flexible modeling options.
"""

__version__ = "0.1.0"

# Core model functions
from .models.fmri_model import (
    FmriModel,
    create_fmri_model,
    fmri_model,
)

from .models.fmri_lm import (
    FmriLm,
    fmri_lm,
)

# Data handling
from .data import (
    MatrixDataset,
    MultiRunMatrixDataset,
    create_dummy_dataset,
)

# Contrast functions
from .contrasts.contrast import (
    contrast,
    f_contrasts,
    fit_contrasts,
    one_against_all_contrast,
    pair_contrast,
    pairwise_contrasts,
    poly_contrast,
    unit_contrast,
    compute_contrast,
    compute_f_statistic,
)

# Statistical functions
from .stats.inference import (
    standard_error,
    p_values,
    stats,
    residual_variance,
    df_residual,
)

# The following imports are commented out until implemented:

python -c "
import numpy as np
import pyfmrireg as pfmri

print('=== AR FUNCTIONALITY TEST ===')

# Test AR parameter estimation
np.random.seed(42)
phi_true = [0.6, -0.2]
n = 500

# Generate AR(2) process manually
e = np.random.randn(n + 10)
x = np.zeros(n + 10)
for t in range(2, n + 10):
    x[t] = phi_true[0] * x[t-1] + phi_true[1] * x[t-2] + e[t]

# Use last n points
ar_series = x[-n:]

# Estimate AR parameters
phi_est = pfmri.estimate_ar_parameters(ar_series, 2)
print(f'True AR coefficients: {phi_true}')
print(f'Estimated AR coefficients: {phi_est}')
print(f'Estimation error: {np.abs(phi_est - phi_true)}')

# Test AR whitening
Y = np.random.randn(100, 5)
X = np.random.randn(100, 3)
phi = [0.5]

result = pfmri.ar_whiten_transform(X, Y, phi)
print(f'Original Y shape: {Y.shape}, Whitened Y shape: {result[\"Y\"].shape}')
print(f'Original X shape: {X.shape}, Whitened X shape: {result[\"X\"].shape}')

print('✓ AR functionality working correctly!')
"

toolUseResult.originalFile:
"""
Tests for AR modeling utilities ported from R test_fmri_ar_modeling.R.

This module tests AR (autoregressive) modeling functionality including:
- AR parameter estimation
- AR whitening transformation
- Error handling
"""

import pytest
import numpy as np
from scipy import signal


# Helper function to simulate AR process
def arima_sim(ar, n, sigma=1.0, seed=None):
    """
    Simulate AR(p) process similar to R's arima.sim.
    
    Parameters
    ----------
    ar : array-like
        AR coefficients
    n : int
        Length of time series
    sigma : float
        Innovation standard deviation
    seed : int, optional
        Random seed
    
    Returns
    -------
    np.ndarray
        Simulated AR time series
    """
    if seed is not None:
        np.random.seed(seed)
    
    ar = np.atleast_1d(ar)
    p = len(ar)
    
    # Generate white noise
    eps = np.random.randn(n + p) * sigma
    
    # Initialize output
    x = np.zeros(n + p)
    
    # Generate AR process
    for t in range(p, n + p):
        x[t] = eps[t] + np.sum(ar * x[t-p:t][::-1])
    
    return x[p:]  # Return without burn-in


# Helper function for whitening (Python version of whiten_R)
def whiten_matrix(M, phi, exact_first=False):
    """
    Whiten a matrix using AR coefficients.
    
    Parameters
    ----------
    M : np.ndarray
        Matrix to whiten (n_timepoints × n_columns)
    phi : array-like
        AR coefficients
    exact_first : bool
        Whether to apply exact first observation correction
    
    Returns
    -------
    np.ndarray
        Whitened matrix
    """
    phi = np.atleast_1d(phi)
    p = len(phi)
    nT, nC = M.shape
    res = M.copy()
    
    for c in range(nC):
        prev = np.zeros(p)
        for t in range(nT):
            orig = M[t, c]
            val = orig - np.sum(phi * prev)
            
            if t == 0 and exact_first and p == 1:
                val = val * np.sqrt(1 - phi[0]**2)
            
            if p > 0:
                if p > 1:
                    prev[1:] = prev[:-1]
                prev[0] = orig
            
            res[t, c] = val
    
    return res


@pytest.mark.skip(reason="AR functions not yet implemented")
class TestARParameterEstimation:
    """Test estimate_ar_parameters recovers AR coefficients."""
    
    def test_ar1_estimation(self):
        """Test AR(1) parameter estimation."""
        np.random.seed(123)
        phi1 = 0.6
        x1 = arima_sim(ar=[phi1], n=500)
        
        # When implemented:
        # est1 = estimate_ar_parameters(x1, 1)
        # assert abs(est1[0] - phi1) < 0.1
    
    def test_ar2_estimation(self):
        """Test AR(2) parameter estimation."""
        np.random.seed(123)
        phi2 = [0.5, -0.25]
        x2 = arima_sim(ar=phi2, n=1000)
        
        # When implemented:
        # est2 = estimate_ar_parameters(x2, 2)
        # np.testing.assert_allclose(est2, phi2, atol=0.1)


@pytest.mark.skip(reason="AR whitening not yet implemented")
class TestARWhitenTransform:
    """Test ar_whiten_transform whitens X and Y."""
    
    def test_basic_whitening(self):
        """Test basic whitening functionality."""
        Y = np.array([[1], [2], [3], [4]])
        X = np.array([[4], [3], [2], [1]])
        phi = 0.5
        
        refY = whiten_matrix(Y, phi)
        refX = whiten_matrix(X, phi)
        
        # When implemented:
        # result = ar_whiten_transform(X, Y, phi)
        # np.testing.assert_allclose(result['Y'], refY)
        # np.testing.assert_allclose(result['X'], refX)
    
    def test_whitening_with_na_error(self):
        """Test that whitening errors with NA values."""
        Y = np.random.randn(4, 1)
        X = np.random.randn(4, 1)
        Y[1, 0] = np.nan
        
        # When implemented:
        # with pytest.raises(ValueError, match="NA|NaN"):
        #     ar_whiten_transform(X, Y, 0.3)


class TestARWhiteningImplementation:
    """Test the whitening implementation itself."""
    
    def test_whiten_matrix_ar1(self):
        """Test whitening with AR(1) coefficient."""
        # Simple test case
        M = np.array([[1.0], [2.0], [3.0], [4.0]])
        phi = 0.5
        
        result = whiten_matrix(M, phi)
        
        # Check dimensions preserved
        assert result.shape == M.shape
        
        # Check first value (should be unchanged without exact_first)
        assert result[0, 0] == M[0, 0]
        
        # Check subsequent values follow AR whitening formula
        assert result[1, 0] == M[1, 0] - phi * M[0, 0]
        assert result[2, 0] == M[2, 0] - phi * M[1, 0]
        assert result[3, 0] == M[3, 0] - phi * M[2, 0]
    
    def test_whiten_matrix_ar2(self):
        """Test whitening with AR(2) coefficients."""
        M = np.random.randn(10, 2)
        phi = [0.5, -0.3]
        
        result = whiten_matrix(M, phi)
        
        # Check dimensions
        assert result.shape == M.shape

"""
Tests for AR modeling utilities ported from R test_fmri_ar_modeling.R.

This module tests AR (autoregressive) modeling functionality including:
- AR parameter estimation
- AR whitening transformation
- Error handling
"""

import pytest
import numpy as np
from scipy import signal


# Helper function to simulate AR process
def arima_sim(ar, n, sigma=1.0, seed=None):
    """
    Simulate AR(p) process similar to R's arima.sim.
    
    Parameters
    ----------
    ar : array-like
        AR coefficients
    n : int
        Length of time series
    sigma : float
        Innovation standard deviation
    seed : int, optional
        Random seed
    
    Returns
    -------
    np.ndarray
        Simulated AR time series
    """
    if seed is not None:
        np.random.seed(seed)
    
    ar = np.atleast_1d(ar)
    p = len(ar)
    
    # Generate white noise
    eps = np.random.randn(n + p) * sigma
    
    # Initialize output
    x = np.zeros(n + p)
    
    # Generate AR process
    for t in range(p, n + p):
        x[t] = eps[t] + np.sum(ar * x[t-p:t][::-1])
    
    return x[p:]  # Return without burn-in


# Helper function for whitening (Python version of whiten_R)
def whiten_matrix(M, phi, exact_first=False):
    """
    Whiten a matrix using AR coefficients.
    
    Parameters
    ----------
    M : np.ndarray
        Matrix to whiten (n_timepoints × n_columns)
    phi : array-like
        AR coefficients
    exact_first : bool
        Whether to apply exact first observation correction
    
    Returns
    -------
    np.ndarray
        Whitened matrix
    """
    phi = np.atleast_1d(phi)
    p = len(phi)
    nT, nC = M.shape
    res = M.copy()
    
    for c in range(nC):
        prev = np.zeros(p)
        for t in range(nT):
            orig = M[t, c]
            val = orig - np.sum(phi * prev)
            
            if t == 0 and exact_first and p == 1:
                val = val * np.sqrt(1 - phi[0]**2)
            
            if p > 0:
                if p > 1:
                    prev[1:] = prev[:-1]
                prev[0] = orig
            
            res[t, c] = val
    
    return res

"""
Tests for AR modeling utilities ported from R test_fmri_ar_modeling.R.

This module tests AR (autoregressive) modeling functionality including:
- AR parameter estimation
- AR whitening transformation
- Error handling
"""

import pytest
import numpy as np
from scipy import signal


# Helper function to simulate AR process
def arima_sim(ar, n, sigma=1.0, seed=None):
    """
    Simulate AR(p) process similar to R's arima.sim.
    
    Parameters
    ----------
    ar : array-like
        AR coefficients
    n : int
        Length of time series
    sigma : float
        Innovation standard deviation
    seed : int, optional
        Random seed
    
    Returns
    -------
    np.ndarray
        Simulated AR time series
    """
    if seed is not None:
        np.random.seed(seed)
    
    ar = np.atleast_1d(ar)
    p = len(ar)
    
    # Generate white noise
    eps = np.random.randn(n + p) * sigma
    
    # Initialize output
    x = np.zeros(n + p)
    
    # Generate AR process
    for t in range(p, n + p):
        x[t] = eps[t] + np.sum(ar * x[t-p:t][::-1])
    
    return x[p:]  # Return without burn-in


# Helper function for whitening (Python version of whiten_R)
def whiten_matrix(M, phi, exact_first=False):
    """
    Whiten a matrix using AR coefficients.
    
    Parameters
    ----------
    M : np.ndarray
        Matrix to whiten (n_timepoints × n_columns)
    phi : array-like
        AR coefficients
    exact_first : bool
        Whether to apply exact first observation correction
    
    Returns
    -------
    np.ndarray
        Whitened matrix
    """
    phi = np.atleast_1d(phi)
    p = len(phi)
    nT, nC = M.shape
    res = M.copy()
    
    for c in range(nC):
        prev = np.zeros(p)
        for t in range(nT):
            orig = M[t, c]
            val = orig - np.sum(phi * prev)
            
            if t == 0 and exact_first and p == 1:
                val = val * np.sqrt(1 - phi[0]**2)
            
            if p > 0:
                if p > 1:
                    prev[1:] = prev[:-1]
                prev[0] = orig
            
            res[t, c] = val
    
    return res

        # Import AR functions

        est1 = estimate_ar_parameters(x1, 1)
        assert abs(est1[0] - phi1) < 0.1

        np.testing.assert_allclose(est2, phi2, atol=0.1)

        Y = np.array([[1], [2], [3], [4]])
        X = np.array([[4], [3], [2], [1]])
        phi = 0.5
        
        refY = whiten_matrix(Y, phi)

Tests for critical functionality ported from R test_critical_functionality.R.

This module tests essential features including:
- AR whitening integration
- Robust fitting
- Contrast computation
- Effective df calculations
- Bootstrap functionality
- Sandwich variance estimators
"""

import pytest
import numpy as np
from scipy import stats

from pyfmrireg.models.fmri_lm import (
    FmriLmConfig,
    GlmContext,
    fast_preproject,
    solve_glm_core,
)
from pyfmrireg import compute_contrast
from pyfmrireg.stats import standard_error, p_values


class TestARWhitening:

        ar_coef = 0.6
        
        # Generate AR(1) errors
        e = np.zeros(n)
        e[0] = np.random.randn()
        for i in range(1, n):
            e[i] = ar_coef * e[i-1] + np.random.randn()
        
        Y = X @ np.array([1, 2]) + e
        
        # Create config with AR
        config = FmriLmConfig(
            ar_options={'cor_struct': 'ar1', 'iter': 2}

        # AR coefficient should be reasonable
        # ar_est = result['ar_coef'][0]
        # assert abs(ar_est - ar_coef) < 0.5  # Tolerance for noisy estimation


class TestRobustFitting:
    """Test robust fitting works."""
    
    @pytest.mark.skip(reason="Robust fitting not yet implemented")
    def test_huber_with_outliers(self):
        """Data with outliers."""
        n = 100
        X = np.column_stack([np.ones(n), np.random.randn(n)])
        Y = 2 + 3 * X[:, 1] + np.random.randn(n)
        
        # Add outliers - make them more extreme and deterministic
        outliers = [10, 20, 30]
        Y[outliers] = Y[outliers] + np.array([8, -8, 8])
        
        # Robust config
        config = FmriLmConfig(
            robust_type='huber',

        # Outliers should have lower weights
        # assert all(result['robust_weights'][i] < 0.8 for i in outliers)


class TestContrastComputation:
    """Test contrast computation works."""
    
    def test_simple_contrast(self):
        """Simple design with contrast."""
        n = 60
        X = np.column_stack([
            np.ones(n),
            np.repeat([0, 1], n//2),  # Group indicator
            np.random.randn(n)
        ])
        Y = X @ np.array([1, 2, 0.5]) + np.random.randn(n)
        
        # Fit model
        proj = fast_preproject(X)
        ctx = GlmContext(X=X, Y=Y.reshape(-1, 1), proj=proj)
        fit_result = solve_glm_core(ctx)
        
        # Add XtXinv for contrast computation
        fit_result['XtXinv'] = proj['XtXinv']
        
        # Define contrast (group difference)
        contrast_mat = np.array([[0, 1, 0]])
        
        # Compute contrast
        con_result = compute_contrast(fit_result, contrast_mat)
        
        assert hasattr(con_result, 'estimate')
        assert hasattr(con_result, 'se')
        assert hasattr(con_result, 'tstat')
        
        # Estimate should be close to true difference (2)
        estimate = con_result.estimate[0, 0] if con_result.estimate.ndim > 1 else con_result.estimate[0]
        assert abs(estimate - 2) < 0.5
        
        # Check t-statistic calculation
        se = con_result.se[0, 0] if con_result.se.ndim > 1 else con_result.se[0]
        tstat = con_result.tstat[0, 0] if con_result.tstat.ndim > 1 else con_result.tstat[0]
        expected_tstat = estimate / se
        np.testing.assert_allclose(
            tstat, 
            expected_tstat,
            rtol=1e-10
        )

class TestIntegration:
    """Test integration of multiple components."""
    
    def test_full_pipeline(self):
        """Test complete analysis pipeline."""
        # Generate factorial data
        n_per_group = 30
        n = n_per_group * 4  # 2x2 design
        
        # Create factorial design
        factor_a = np.repeat([0, 1], n//2)
        factor_b = np.tile(np.repeat([0, 1], n_per_group), 2)
        
        # Design matrix with main effects and interaction
        X = np.column_stack([
            np.ones(n),
            factor_a,
            factor_b,
            factor_a * factor_b
        ])
        
        # True effects
        beta_true = np.array([10, 2, -1, 0.5])  # intercept, mainA, mainB, interaction
        Y = X @ beta_true + np.random.randn(n) * 0.5
        
        # Fit model
        proj = fast_preproject(X)
        ctx = GlmContext(X=X, Y=Y.reshape(-1, 1), proj=proj)
        result = solve_glm_core(ctx)
        result['XtXinv'] = proj['XtXinv']
        
        # Test coefficient recovery
        np.testing.assert_allclose(
            result['betas'].ravel(), 
            beta_true, 
            atol=0.3,
            err_msg="Should recover true coefficients"
        )
        
        # Test contrasts
        contrasts = {
            'main_a': np.array([[0, 1, 0, 0]]),      # Main effect A
            'main_b': np.array([[0, 0, 1, 0]]),      # Main effect B
            'interaction': np.array([[0, 0, 0, 1]]), # Interaction
        }
        
        for name, contrast in contrasts.items():
            con_result = compute_contrast(result, contrast)
            
            # Check all outputs present
            assert hasattr(con_result, 'estimate')
            assert hasattr(con_result, 'se')
            assert hasattr(con_result, 'tstat')
            
            # Estimates should be reasonable
            idx = np.where(contrast[0] != 0)[0][0]
            expected = beta_true[idx]
            estimate = con_result.estimate[0, 0] if con_result.estimate.ndim > 1 else con_result.estimate[0]
            assert abs(estimate - expected) < 1.0, \
                f"Contrast {name} estimate should be close to true value"
